mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Nepal on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia. Nepal is a small landlocked country in the Himalayas, between India and China 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Nepal?
mapQuestions.answer: Kathmandu, a city in a high valley surrounded by mountains.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain on earth, and where is it?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Everest, 8,849 metres, on the border between Nepal and Tibet (China). Nepalis call it "Sagarmatha", "head of the sky".

mapQuestions.question: Why is the Nepalese flag a strange shape?
mapQuestions.answer: It is the only national flag in the world that is not a rectangle. It is two stacked triangles, representing the Himalayan peaks, with the sun and the moon on them.

mapQuestions.question: How many of the world's 10 highest mountains are in Nepal?
mapQuestions.answer: Eight of them. Including Everest, Kangchenjunga, Lhotse, Makalu, Cho Oyu, Dhaulagiri, Manaslu and Annapurna.

phrases.english: Hello / I bow to the divine in you

foods.name: Dal bhat
foods.description: A pile of rice (bhat) with lentil soup (dal), and a few vegetable curries and pickles. Eaten twice a day by most Nepalis, refills are usually free. There is a saying: "dal bhat power, 24 hour".

foods.name: Momo
foods.description: Steamed or fried dumplings filled with minced buffalo, chicken or vegetables, served with a fiery tomato dipping sauce. Originally from Tibet, now Nepal's most loved street food.

foods.name: Sel roti
foods.description: A ring-shaped doughnut made from rice batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy. Traditional snack at festivals like Tihar (the festival of lights).

foods.name: Newari samay baji
foods.description: A traditional Newar feast plate: beaten flat rice, marinated buffalo, beans, eggs, potato salad and chutneys, all served on a single brass plate. From the Kathmandu Valley.

foods.name: Chhoyla
foods.description: Spicy grilled buffalo meat marinated in mustard oil, chilli, ginger and Sichuan peppercorns. A Newar speciality, usually eaten as a snack with a glass of homemade rice beer.

foods.name: Yomari
foods.description: Steamed sweet dumplings made from rice flour, shaped like a fig, filled with molasses and sesame paste. Made by the Newar people every year for the Yomari Punhi full-moon festival.

scavengerItems.label: A line of coloured prayer flags fluttering in the wind
A "stupa" (white dome-shaped Buddhist shrine) with watchful eyes painted on it
A yak or a yak-cow hybrid (a "dzo") carrying goods on a high mountain trail
A long suspension bridge swaying over a river
A monkey at a temple (especially at Swayambhunath, the "Monkey Temple")
Someone spinning a prayer wheel as they walk past
Steam rising from a momo stall on a chilly morning
A snow-capped mountain peeking out behind a closer hill
The sunrise hitting the snow peaks while everything below is still in shade
A red "tika" (powder dot) on someone's forehead
A "dhaka topi" (traditional patterned Nepali hat) on an older man
Mani stones, carved with Buddhist prayers, stacked in a wall along a trail

geoMatches.description: The capital. Old Buddhist and Hindu temples, narrow medieval streets in Patan and Bhaktapur, and the smell of incense everywhere.
A peaceful lakeside town surrounded by the Annapurna mountain range. Paragliders fly off the cliffs into thermal currents above the lake.
In the Khumbu valley. The Sherpa villages of Namche Bazaar and Tengboche, and the trail to Everest Base Camp, with the world's tallest mountain looming above.
One of the world's most famous trekking routes. 200km loop around the Annapurna massif, crossing a 5,400m pass between two glaciers.
A subtropical jungle national park in the south. Home to one-horned rhinos, Bengal tigers, gharial crocodiles and Asian elephants.
A small town near the Indian border, where Siddhartha Gautama (who became the Buddha) was born around 2,500 years ago.

senses.smell: Incense and butter lamps at temples, momos steaming on the street, woodsmoke from village kitchens, mountain pine, the dry dust of the trails, marigold garlands…
senses.hear: Buddhist prayer chants ("Om mani padme hum"), monastery horns booming across valleys, prayer flags snapping in the wind, "Namaste!", the rush of a glacial river…
senses.taste: Hot dal bhat eaten with the right hand, smoky momo dipping sauce, sweet milky chiya tea with cardamom, the cool of altitude on your tongue, sticky yomari…
senses.feel: Thin cold air at altitude (you breathe twice as often), strong sun, the sway of a suspension bridge, soft yak wool, warm sun on cold mornings, woodsmoke on clothes…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How do you climb the highest mountain on earth?
stories.body: Mount Everest is 8,849 metres tall, taller than any plane flies. The air at the top has less than a third of the oxygen at sea level, which is why most climbers carry oxygen bottles. In 1953, a New Zealander named Edmund Hillary and a Sherpa named Tenzing Norgay became the first known people to stand on the summit. They posed for photos and then quickly came back down because they had only minutes before they would run out of oxygen. Today around 600 people summit Everest each year. Some die trying. The climbers are guided by Sherpas, the local mountain people who can climb at altitude where most humans would pass out within an hour.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could climb to the top of any mountain in the world (and definitely make it back), which one and why?

stories.question: Why are there coloured flags everywhere?
stories.body: In Nepal and Tibet, you will see strings of colourful square flags fluttering on every bridge, mountain pass, temple roof and trail. They are Buddhist prayer flags. There are five colours in a set: blue (sky), white (air), red (fire), green (water) and yellow (earth). Each flag is printed with a prayer, and the belief is that as the wind blows the flags, it carries the prayers out across the world for everyone's benefit. The flags fade and fray over time, which is meant to happen. The prayers go out and the flag eventually returns to nature, while a new set is hung in its place.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could send one wish out into the wind every time it blew, what would your wish be?

stories.question: Why do Sherpas have superpowers?
stories.body: The Sherpa are an ethnic group who have lived in the high mountains of Nepal for around 500 years, originally from Tibet. Most live above 2,500m. Over generations, their bodies have adapted in remarkable ways: their blood carries oxygen more efficiently than other people's, their hearts pump differently, and they have a slightly different version of a gene called EPAS1. The result: a Sherpa can carry double their own body weight up to Everest Base Camp without getting altitude sickness, while a tourist may struggle just to walk slowly. The word "Sherpa" has become so famous for high-altitude work that it is now used to describe any expert mountain guide anywhere in the world.

stories.question: What is a living goddess?
stories.body: In Kathmandu lives a girl called the Kumari, who is worshipped as a living goddess. She is chosen at the age of about 4 from a small Buddhist Newar community, after passing many tests (no scars, no missing teeth, the right horoscope, and a series of rituals to test fearlessness). She then lives in a special palace called the Kumari Ghar, dressed in red, with a third eye painted on her forehead. She rarely speaks in public, never touches the ground outside, and is brought out at her palace window to bless visitors. When she reaches puberty, she becomes "normal" again and a new Kumari is chosen. Old Kumaris go on to ordinary lives, though many find it surprisingly hard.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Would you want to be a child considered a goddess for a few years if it meant you had to give up playing outside and going to school? Why or why not?

stories.question: What was the 2015 Nepal earthquake?
stories.body: On Saturday 25 April 2015, just before noon, a 7.8 magnitude earthquake shook Nepal for about 50 seconds. Nearly 9,000 people died, around 22,000 were injured, and 600,000 homes were destroyed. Ancient temples in Kathmandu, Bhaktapur and Patan that had stood for hundreds of years collapsed in seconds. On Everest, an avalanche killed 21 climbers at Base Camp. Then a second large earthquake hit two weeks later. The country had to rebuild more or less from scratch, with help from around the world. Many temples have been carefully reconstructed using the same stones, often by master craftsmen using techniques passed down for generations.

convoQuestions.question: How is the altitude making you feel? Is your head a bit funny?
What's the best dal bhat you've had so far?
What's one thing that surprises you about Nepal compared to home?
If you had to choose: trek for 10 days in the mountains, or paddle a canoe through Chitwan jungle?
Have you seen a real Himalayan peak with your own eyes yet? How did you feel?
Nepalis live among the biggest mountains on earth and yet most of them have never climbed Everest. Why do you think we sometimes ignore amazing things on our own doorstep?

wordSearch.hint: Nepali foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Water buffalo
animals.description: Ploughing terraced fields in the hills and ferrying loads through villages.

animals.name: Sacred cow
animals.description: Like in India, cows wander Kathmandu freely — never harmed.

animals.name: Rhesus macaque
animals.description: Especially at the "Monkey Temple" (Swayambhunath) in Kathmandu.

animals.name: One-horned rhino
animals.description: In Chitwan National Park — over 700 of them roam the grasslands and rivers.

animals.name: Bengal tiger
animals.description: Chitwan has a healthy tiger population, but they're elusive — go on a multi-day safari to maximise chances.

animals.name: Snow leopard
animals.description: In the highest Himalayan valleys (Dolpo, Mustang). Almost no one sees one.
